title: Net-Zero Claim Validation Standard
content: Any net-zero or carbon neutrality claim must include: (1) a defined baseline year with quantified Scope 1+2 emissions, (2) an interim 2030 target aligned to a 1.5°C pathway, (3) third-party assurance from an accredited body (e.g. Bureau Veritas, SGS, EY), and (4) a published transition plan with milestones. Claims without all four elements should be flagged as High greenwashing risk. Unverified claims are the primary driver of enforcement actions under ISSB S2 and GRI 305.

title: Carbon Intensity Benchmarks by Sector
content: Industry-average carbon intensity benchmarks (tCO2e per $M revenue): Energy/Oil & Gas: 450, Mining: 350, Manufacturing: 220, Agriculture: 130, Real Estate: 80, Finance: 40, Technology: 50, Logistics/Shipping: 110, Retail: 60, Healthcare: 45. Companies above 2× the sector benchmark should receive a High transition risk rating. Companies below 0.5× the benchmark with verified reduction trajectories qualify for ESG grade uplift.

title: Scope 3 Materiality Threshold
content: For most sectors, Scope 3 (value chain) emissions represent 70–90% of total GHG footprint. Scope 3 reporting is material and mandatory under ISSB S2 para 29 for any company with material upstream or downstream activities. Omitting Scope 3 while claiming climate leadership or net-zero status is a critical greenwashing indicator. Minimum required categories: purchased goods & services (Cat 1), capital goods (Cat 2), fuel & energy (Cat 3), upstream transport (Cat 4), use of sold products (Cat 11).

title: NGFS Scenario Physical Risk Thresholds
content: Under NGFS scenarios: Net Zero 2050 (1.5°C) — transition risk peaks 2025–2035 at carbon price $130+/tCO2e; physical risk contained at 1.45°C warming by 2050. Delayed Transition (2°C) — carbon price reaches $40 by 2030, $300 by 2050; physical risk moderate at 1.38°C warming. Current Policies (3°C+) — carbon price stays low ($28 by 2030); chronic physical risk severe, sea level rise +0.5m by 2100, extreme heat events 5× more frequent. Assets in coastal zones, flood plains, or heat-stressed agriculture regions face stranded asset risk under 3°C+ scenarios.

title: ERI (Environmental Readiness Index) Scoring Logic
content: ERI Score 0–100 tiering: 85–100 = Climate Leader (Tier 1): Net-zero committed, SBTI-approved, full Scope 1/2/3 disclosure, third-party assured, transition plan published. 70–84 = Advanced Reporter (Tier 2): Comprehensive disclosure, 2030 targets set, most framework requirements met. 55–69 = Developing Reporter (Tier 3): Core emissions disclosed, partial framework alignment, gaps in Scope 3 and governance. 40–54 = Early Stage (Tier 4): Basic disclosure only, no verified targets. Below 40 = Pre-Disclosure (Tier 5): Minimal or no ESG disclosure. Grade mapping: Tier 1 = A/A+, Tier 2 = B+/A-, Tier 3 = B/B-, Tier 4 = C/C+, Tier 5 = D.

title: CSRD Mandatory Disclosure Requirements (EU)
content: CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive) applies to: EU companies with >250 employees OR >€40M turnover OR >€20M balance sheet (from FY2024 for large companies, FY2026 for listed SMEs). Required disclosures: double materiality assessment (financial + impact materiality), ESRS E1 (climate change — mandatory for all), ESRS S1 (own workforce), ESRS G1 (governance). Non-EU companies with >€150M EU-net turnover must comply from FY2028. Third-party limited assurance required from FY2024, reasonable assurance from FY2028.

title: Board-Level Governance Indicators
content: Strong ESG governance requires: (1) Named board committee with climate oversight mandate, (2) ESG/sustainability KPIs linked to executive remuneration (minimum 20% weighting for Tier 1 companies), (3) Annual board-level review of climate risk (TCFD Governance pillar), (4) Chief Sustainability Officer (CSO) or equivalent at C-suite level, (5) Published climate risk management process. Absence of board-level accountability is a key governance risk flag and reduces the governance pillar score by 20–30 points. Best practice: separate sustainability committee with independent chair.

title: SBTi Science-Based Target Validation
content: Science-based targets must be validated by the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) to be credible. Self-declared 'science-based' claims without SBTi validation or equivalent (e.g. CDP A-list alignment) are a greenwashing flag. SBTi requires: near-term targets (5–10 year horizon, 1.5°C aligned), long-term net-zero target (by 2050 at latest), Scope 3 included if >40% of total emissions. Financial institutions must use FINZ (Financial Sector Science-Based Targets Guidance). Companies with SBTi-approved targets receive a +10 point ESG score uplift.

title: Supply Chain Climate Risk Cascade Rules
content: Supply chain climate risk propagates upstream and downstream. High dependency sectors (Logistics 90%, Manufacturing 80%, Energy 70%): a critical physical risk event at a Tier 1 supplier increases portfolio-level risk by 15–25 points. Key cascade triggers: port disruption (flood/cyclone) → manufacturing stoppage within 72 hours; energy price spike (carbon tax) → manufacturing margin compression 8–15%; agricultural drought → food processing and retail supply disruption. Companies without supplier climate risk assessments or supplier engagement programs should receive a supply chain risk flag.

title: Renewable Energy Transition Benchmark
content: Renewable energy procurement standards: RE100 commitment (100% renewable electricity by 2040) qualifies for Environmental score uplift. Current benchmarks: leading companies in technology/finance sector average 85%+ renewable electricity; manufacturing sector average 42%; energy sector average 28%. Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) and Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs) both qualify. Scope 2 market-based emissions must be reported separately from location-based. Companies without a renewable energy target and currently below 30% renewable electricity face a medium-to-high transition risk rating.
